title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.title: Pathologic Hallmarks of ALS
items.content: ALS involves the progressive degeneration of both upper and lower motor neurons, which is essential for diagnosis. The disease also features cytoskeletal abnormalities in motor neurons early on.
keyPoints: Death of both upper (corticospinal) and lower motor neurons is required to diagnose ALS.
Early involvement includes focal enlargements or 'spheroids' in proximal motor axons, composed of neurofilaments and other proteins.
Ubiquitin-positive aggregates often associated with TDP43 protein are common in both sporadic and familial ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.title: Motor Neuron Disease Selectivity in ALS
items.content: ALS selectively affects motor neurons, sparing sensory apparatus and cerebellar structures. However, there is evidence of neuronal dysfunction beyond the motor system.
keyPoints: Sensory systems and cerebellar structures remain intact under light microscopy.
Cognitive processing components are preserved except in cases with frontotemporal dementia.
Neuronal dysfunction outside the motor system indicated by glucose metabolism studies.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.title: Muscle Changes in ALS Pathology
items.content: ALS leads to muscle denervation, atrophy, and reinnervation attempts. The disease's impact on the corticospinal tracts results in lateral sclerosis.
keyPoints: Denervation of peripheral motor neurons causes muscle fiber atrophy, visible in biopsies and clinical exams.
Early phases show limited reinnervation by nearby distal motor nerve terminals compared to other disorders like poliomyelitis.
Loss of cortical motor neurons leads to thinning of corticospinal tracts, causing firmness due to fibrillary gliosis (lateral sclerosis).
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Initial Presentation of ALS: Lower vs Upper Motor Neuron Involvement
items.content: ALS can initially present with either lower or upper motor neuron dysfunction. Lower motor neuron involvement typically manifests as asymmetric weakness, cramping during volitional movements (especially in the morning), muscle wasting, atrophy, and fasciculations. Conversely, corticospinal involvement presents with hyperactive reflexes, spasticity, muscle stiffness, dysarthria, and pseudobulbar affect.
keyPoints: Lower motor neuron dysfunction: asymmetric weakness, cramping in the morning, muscle wasting, atrophy, fasciculations.
Upper motor neuron involvement: hyperactive reflexes, spastic resistance, muscle stiffness, dysarthria, pseudobulbar affect.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Progression and Symmetry in ALS
items.content: ALS progresses from initial asymmetric involvement to a symmetric distribution across all muscle groups. Both upper and lower motor neurons are eventually implicated, regardless of the initial site of disease. Sensory, bowel, bladder, and cognitive functions remain preserved until late stages.
keyPoints: Progression: Initial asymmetry to eventual symmetry in muscle involvement.
Preserved functions: Sensory, bowel, bladder, cognitive functions intact until late stages.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Biomarkers for ALS
items.content: Diagnosis of ALS requires simultaneous upper and lower motor neuron involvement with progressive weakness. The World Federation of Neurology guidelines classify ALS as 'definite' if three or four regions (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral) are involved; 'probable' with two sites; 'possible' with one site. Elevated neurofilament light chains in CSF and serum correlate with disease aggression and progression.
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria: Simultaneous upper/lower motor neuron involvement, progressive weakness.
Classification: Definite (3-4 regions), Probable (2 sites), Possible (1 site).
Biomarkers: Elevated neurofilament light chains indicate aggressive disease progression.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Genetic Basis of Familial ALS
items.content: Familial ALS (FALS) accounts for about 10% of all ALS cases, inherited as an autosomal dominant trait. It is clinically indistinguishable from sporadic ALS but involves specific genetic mutations.
keyPoints: FALS involves both corticospinal and lower motor neurons.
Genetic studies have identified key genes: C9orf72 (~45–50% of FALS), SOD1 (20%), TDP43 and FUS/TLS (each ~5%).
C9orf72 mutations also account for 5–10% of sporadic ALS cases.
Rare gene mutations can cause ALS-like diseases, such as those in senataxin causing a slowly evolving ALS variant.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.title: Epidemiological Insights into ALS Prevalence and Risk Factors
items.content: ALS has an incidence of 1–3 per 100,000 and a prevalence of 3–5 per 100,000. It accounts for about 1 in 500 deaths in North America and Western Europe.
keyPoints: Median survival from ALS is 3 to 5 years.
Incidence: 1–3 per 100,000; Prevalence: 3–5 per 100,000.
Risk factors include exposure to pesticides, silica, smoking, and military service.
>500,000 individuals in the U.S. are predicted to die from ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.title: Exclusion of Reversible Causes in ALS Diagnosis
items.content: ALS diagnosis necessitates ruling out potentially treatable conditions that mimic its symptoms, especially when presentation is atypical. This includes distinguishing from cervical spinal cord compression and multifocal motor neuropathy with conduction block (MMCB).
keyPoints: Exclude reparable causes of motor neuron dysfunction.
Atypical cases: restriction to upper or lower motor neurons, involvement of non-motor neurons, or evidence of motor neuronal conduction block.
Cervical spinal cord compression mimics ALS but lacks cranial nerve involvement and sensory changes; MRI can aid differentiation.
MMCB should be considered in differential diagnosis.
Hematopoietic disorders like lymphoma may mimic ALS; presence of M-component suggests bone marrow biopsy.
Lyme disease, lead poisoning, thyrotoxicosis, and genetic disorders (e.g., C9orf72) are other considerations.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Identifying ALS Mimics through Clinical Features and Tests
items.content: Certain clinical features and tests can help differentiate ALS from its mimics, such as the absence of pain or sensory changes, normal CSF, and specific genetic testing.
keyPoints: Absence of cranial nerve involvement may suggest ALS over cervical lesions.
Normal bowel/bladder function, radiologic studies, and CSF favor ALS diagnosis.
Genetic tests for C9orf72, SOD1, TDP43, FUS/TLS, and hexosaminidase A are crucial in differential diagnosis.
Benign fasciculations can be differentiated from ALS by lack of weakness or atrophy on electrophysiologic examination.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Overlap and Atypical Presentations in ALS and Related Disorders
items.content: ALS may present concurrently with cognitive impairments or other neurodegenerative features, necessitating consideration of overlapping syndromes like FTD.
keyPoints: 15% of clinically pure ALS cases show subtle cognitive impairment detectable by neuropsychological testing.
C9orf72 mutations link ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) in up to 40% of FTD cases.
Atypical phenotypes may include amyotrophy with parkinsonism-like features due to tau protein mutations.
Chronic traumatic encephalopathy can present with an ALS-like disorder, associated with TDP43 and neurofibrillary tangles.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Genetic Categories and Mechanisms of ALS Pathogenesis
items.content: ALS pathogenesis can be categorized into three genetic groups based on the primary molecular dysfunction: protein instability, RNA processing perturbations, and axonal cytoskeleton defects. Each category involves distinct mechanisms that impair motor neuron viability.
keyPoints: Three categories of ALS genes: 1) Protein degradation issues (SOD1, ubiquilin-1/2, p62), 2) RNA processing disruptions (C9orf73, TDP43, FUS), 3) Axonal cytoskeleton and transport defects (dynactin, profilin-1).
C9orf72 involves an expansion of a hexanucleotide repeat (-GGGGCC-) beyond the normal upper limit of 30 repeats to hundreds or thousands.
Dipeptides from C9orf72 RNA expansions are unique biomarkers detectable in spinal fluid.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Role of Nonneuronal Cells and Protein Aggregates in ALS
items.content: Activated nonneuronal cells like microglia and astrocytes influence ALS progression, particularly in transgenic models. Additionally, protein aggregates from familial gene defects are implicated in sporadic ALS cases.
keyPoints: Microglia and astrocytes impact disease course in ALS-transgenic mice.
Protein aggregates (e.g., SOD1) found in spinal cord of sporadic ALS without mutations suggest posttranslational modifications may be pathogenic.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Complexity of Neuronal Cell Death in ALS
items.content: Neuronal cell death in ALS involves multiple cellular processes across different motor neuron components, including excitotoxicity, defective autophagy, and mitochondrial dysfunction.
keyPoints: Cell death mechanisms include excitotoxicity, impaired axonal transport, oxidative stress, endoplasmic reticulum stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction.
Hexanucleotide expansions in C9orf72 disrupt nucleocytoplasmic transport, highlighting the role of mRNA export genes like GLE1.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Riluzole's Role in ALS Management
items.content: Riluzole, the only drug approved for ALS, modestly extends survival by potentially reducing excitotoxicity through decreased glutamate release. It is generally well tolerated but can cause nausea, dizziness, weight loss, and elevated liver enzymes.
keyPoints: Drug: Riluzole
Dosage: 100 mg/d
Effect on Survival: Modest lengthening; trial showed survival rate at 18 months with riluzole similar to placebo at 15 months.
Mechanism: May reduce excitotoxicity by diminishing glutamate release.
Side Effects: Nausea, dizziness, weight loss, elevated liver enzymes.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.title: Edaravone's Use in ALS Treatment
items.content: Edaravone, approved by the FDA for ALS, shows a modest reduction in disease progression on an ALS disability scale but does not impact survival. It acts as an antioxidant and is administered intravenously or orally.
keyPoints: Drug: Edaravone
Approval Basis: Single 6-month study showing modest reduction in worsening on ALS disability scale; survival not included as endpoint.
Mechanism: Believed to act as an antioxidant.
Administration: Initially via recurring monthly 10-day series of daily intravenous infusions, now available orally.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.title: Distinguishing X-Linked Spinobulbar Muscular Atrophy from ALS
items.content: X-linked spinobulbar muscular atrophy (Kennedy's Disease) is differentiated from ALS by the absence of pyramidal tract signs and presence of gynecomastia. It involves progressive weakness due to an expanded CAG repeat in the androgen receptor gene.
keyPoints: Progressive limb and bulbar muscle weakness
Gynecomastia and reduced fertility
Absence of spasticity (pyramidal tract signs)
Presence of subtle sensory neuropathy
Expanded CAG repeat in the androgen receptor gene
Inverse correlation between number of CAG repeats and age of onset
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
items.content: Spinal muscular atrophy is characterized by loss of SMN protein affecting RNA trafficking. SMA I has the earliest onset with rapid progression, while SMA III shows proximal muscle weakness. Treatments like nusinersen and AAV gene therapy have shown benefits.
keyPoints: Loss of survival motor neuron (SMN) protein
Neuropathological loss of large motor neurons
Infantile SMA (SMA I): earliest onset, rapid progression, death within first year
Juvenile SMA (SMA III): late childhood onset, proximal muscle weakness
Electrophysiologic and biopsy evidence distinguish from myopathies
Treatments: nusinersen alters mRNA splicing; AAV delivers SMN gene
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.title: Identifying Multifocal Motor Neuropathy with Conduction Block (MMCB)
items.content: MMCB is characterized by focal conduction blocks in motor neurons, often associated with antibodies to GM1. Unlike ALS, MMCB responds well to treatments like IV immunoglobulin or chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Regionally disrupted lower motor neuron function
Elevated serum titers of antibodies to ganglioside GM1
Hypothesized focal paranodal demyelination
Absence of corticospinal signs
Responsive to IV immunoglobulin or chemotherapy
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of Primary Lateral Sclerosis (PLS)
items.content: Primary Lateral Sclerosis is characterized by progressive spastic weakness, often with involvement of the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts. It lacks fasciculations and amyotrophy, distinguishing it from ALS. Diagnostic considerations include differentiating PLS from multiple sclerosis or adult-onset spastic paraplegia.
keyPoints: Progressive spastic weakness of limbs
Spastic dysarthria and dysphagia may occur
Fasciculations, amyotrophy, sensory changes absent
Electromyography and muscle biopsy show no denervation
Selective loss of large pyramidal cells in the precentral gyrus
Differential diagnosis includes multiple sclerosis, other demyelinating diseases, adult-onset spastic paraplegia
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.title: Characteristics and Genetic Basis of Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP)
items.content: Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia is often autosomal dominant, with over 80 genetic types identified. Symptoms typically begin in the third or fourth decade, presenting as progressive spastic weakness starting in the lower extremities. Complex forms may involve other nervous system regions.
keyPoints: >80 genetic types of HSP
Causative mutations in >60 genes
Symptoms often start in the third or fourth decade
Progressive spastic weakness begins in lower extremities
Spastin gene commonly implicated in dominant forms
Atlastin gene associated with childhood-onset dominant form
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.title: Pathology and Genetic Variants of Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP)
items.content: Neuropathologically, HSP shows degeneration of corticospinal tracts with a dying-back pattern. Various genetic mutations cause both dominant and recessive forms, including spastin for dominant and myelin proteolipid protein for X-linked recessive cases.
keyPoints: Degeneration of cortico-spinal tracts
Dying-back or distal axonopathy in CNS
Spastin gene implicated in dominantly inherited HSP
Myelin proteolipid protein mutations cause X-linked recessive HSP
Paraplegin gene defects linked to mitochondrial function
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is a pathologic hallmark of motor neuron degenerative disorders?
options: Death of both upper and lower motor neurons.
items.explanation: The hallmark is the death of both lower motor neurons (anterior horn cells and brainstem homologues) and upper motor neurons (originating in layer five of the motor cortex).
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.question: In ALS, what is typically affected early in the illness?
options: Parasympathetic neurons in the sacral spinal cord.
items.explanation: In ALS, the motor neuron cytoskeleton is typically affected early in the illness.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following motor neuron diseases affects only lower motor neurons?
items.explanation: In bulbar palsy, the lower motor neurons of brainstem are most severely involved.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.question: What is a common feature seen in both sporadic and familial ALS?
items.explanation: In both sporadic and familial ALS, there is commonly proliferation of astroglia and microglia.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is typically the first evidence of ALS when lower motor neuron dysfunction and early denervation occur?
options: Insidiously developing asymmetric weakness, usually distally in one limb
Difficulty with chewing, swallowing, and movements of the face and tongue
Severe brainstem disease leading to loss of ocular motility
items.explanation: The first evidence is typically insidiously developing asymmetric weakness distally in one limb.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which symptom is associated with prominent corticospinal involvement in ALS?
options: Spontaneous twitching of motor units, or fasciculations
Hyperactivity of the muscle-stretch reflexes (tendon jerks)
items.explanation: Prominent corticospinal involvement leads to hyperactivity of the muscle-stretch reflexes.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of ALS regarding motor neuron involvement?
options: Only upper or lower motor neurons are involved, not both
Both upper and lower motor neurons will eventually be implicated
Sensory functions are impaired early in the disease
Ocular motility is affected early in the disease
items.explanation: It is characteristic of ALS that both upper and lower motor neurons will eventually be involved.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which biomarkers are recognized as clinical manifestations in most cases of ALS?
options: Elevated levels of neurofilament light chains (Nfl) or phosphorylated neurofilament heavy chains
Decreased levels of serum creatine kinase
Reduced levels of monocyte chemoattractant protein 1
Lower levels of acetylcholine receptor antibodies
items.explanation: Elevated levels of neurofilament light chains or phosphorylated neurofilament heavy chains are recognized biomarkers.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which genetic mutation accounts for the highest percentage of familial ALS cases?
items.explanation: Mutations in C9orf72 account for ~45–50% of familial ALS cases.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.question: What is the most common mode of inheritance for familial ALS?
items.explanation: Familial ALS is inherited as an autosomal dominant trait.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.question: Which of the following genes is NOT mentioned as a common cause of familial ALS?
items.explanation: BRCA1 is not mentioned in the passage as a gene associated with familial ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.question: Which condition can mimic ALS by causing weakness, wasting, fasciculations in the upper limbs, and spasticity in the legs?
options: Compression of the cervical spinal cord or cervicomedullary junction from tumors or spondylosis
Multifocal motor neuropathy with conduction block (MMCB)
items.explanation: Compression of the cervical spinal cord or cervicomedullary junction from tumors or spondylosis can produce symptoms closely resembling ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a key distinguishing feature between ALS and foramen magnum lesions?
options: Presence of intense proximal limb pain
Involvement of cranial nerves, particularly the twelfth (hypoglossal) nerve
items.explanation: Foramen magnum lesions may involve the hypoglossal nerve, causing tongue paralysis, which is not typical in ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which disorder should be considered when a lower motor axonal neuropathy evolves with hematopoietic disorders?
options: Multifocal motor neuropathy with conduction block (MMCB)
items.explanation: A lower motor axonal neuropathy associated with hematopoietic disorders like lymphoma or multiple myeloma should prompt consideration of these conditions.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which genetic mutation is commonly associated with both ALS and frontotemporal dementia (FTD)?
items.explanation: The C9orf72 mutation is associated with both ALS and FTD, reflecting shared embryologic origins.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is associated with the genetic causes of ALS related to protein instability?
options: Perturbations in RNA processing, transport, and metabolism
Instability of mutant proteins such as SOD1
items.explanation: The primary problem in one group of ALS genetic causes is the inherent instability of mutant proteins like SOD1.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What unique biomarker is associated with C9orf72 ALS?
options: Expanded RNA repeats forming intranuclear foci
Lengthy dipeptides detected in spinal fluid
items.explanation: C9orf72 ALS is characterized by lengthy dipeptides that are unique biomarkers and can be detected in the spinal fluid.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which protein's mutations may lead to motor neuron cell death through loss of function or toxic gain of function?
items.explanation: Mutations in FUS/TLS may provoke motor neuron cell death through loss of function or toxic gain of function.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which cellular process is NOT mentioned as contributing to the ultimate neuronal cell death in ALS?
items.explanation: The passage mentions excitotoxicity, defective autophagy, and impairment of axonal transport as contributors to neuronal cell death, but not enhanced protein synthesis.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which drug approved for ALS has been shown to modestly lengthen survival by potentially reducing excitotoxicity?
items.explanation: Riluzole has been approved for ALS and may reduce excitotoxicity by diminishing glutamate release.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.question: What is the primary mechanism believed to be responsible for edaravone's action in ALS treatment?
items.explanation: Edaravone is believed to act as an antioxidant in ALS treatment.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.question: Which intervention has been FDA approved specifically for SOD1-mediated ALS?
items.explanation: Tofersen, an antisense oligonucleotide that suppresses SOD1 expression, is FDA approved for SOD1-mediated ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.question: Which of the following is a distinguishing feature between X-Linked Spinobulbar Muscular Atrophy and ALS?
options: Presence of spasticity in all patients
Absence of signs of pyramidal tract disease (spasticity)
items.explanation: X-Linked Spinobulbar Muscular Atrophy is distinguished from ALS by the absence of spasticity.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.question: What molecular defect underlies X-Linked Spinobulbar Muscular Atrophy?
options: Expanded trinucleotide repeat (CAG) in the androgen receptor gene
items.explanation: The molecular defect is an expanded trinucleotide repeat (CAG) in the first exon of the androgen receptor gene.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.question: Which treatment has shown dramatic benefit in infantile SMA by altering mRNA splicing?
items.explanation: Nusinersen is a treatment that alters mRNA splicing to generate sufficient normal SMN protein.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.question: What is the primary defect in most families with Spinal Muscular Atrophy?
options: Elevated serum titers of antibodies to ganglioside GM1
items.explanation: The primary defect is the loss of a protein (SMN) important for RNA complex formation and trafficking.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.question: Which disorder is characterized by progressive spastic weakness of the limbs without fasciculations, amyotrophy, or sensory changes?
items.explanation: Primary Lateral Sclerosis is characterized by progressive spastic weakness without fasciculations, amyotrophy, or sensory changes.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.question: What gene mutation is most commonly implicated in dominantly inherited Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP)?
items.explanation: The spastin gene, which encodes a microtubule interacting protein, is most commonly implicated in dominantly inherited HSP.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.question: Which of the following conditions can be confused with Primary Lateral Sclerosis (PLS) early in its course?
items.explanation: Early in its course, PLS raises the question of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases as diagnostic considerations.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.question: What is a common feature of Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP) in its pure form?
options: Spastic leg weakness with posterior column abnormalities
Elevated serum levels of very-long-chain fatty acids
items.explanation: In pure forms of HSP, spastic leg weakness is often accompanied by posterior column (vibration and position) abnormalities.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.statement: In ALS, the entire sensory apparatus remains intact.
items.explanation: By light microscopy, the entire sensory apparatus remains intact in ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.statement: In ALS, motor neurons required for ocular motility are affected.
items.explanation: Motor neurons required for ocular motility remain unaffected in ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: In ALS, sensory functions are preserved even in the late stages of the illness.
items.explanation: Sensory, bowel and bladder, and cognitive functions are preserved even in the late stages.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Ocular motility is affected early in ALS when there is severe brainstem disease.
items.explanation: Ocular motility is spared until very late stages of the illness.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Mutations in C9orf72 are responsible for about 5–10% of sporadic ALS cases.
items.explanation: The passage states that mutations in C9orf72 account for perhaps 5–10% of sporadic ALS cases.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.statement: Familial ALS is clinically distinguishable from sporadic ALS.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that familial ALS is clinically indistinguishable from sporadic ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · EPIDEMIOLOGY | FAMILIAL ALS

items.statement: Benign fasciculations can be distinguished from those in ALS by the presence of weakness or atrophy.
items.explanation: The absence, not presence, of weakness and atrophy on electrophysiologic examination usually excludes serious neurologic disease like ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Up to 40% of FTD cases are associated with mutations in the C9orf72 gene.
items.explanation: A significant proportion of FTD cases, up to 40%, harbor mutations in the C9orf72 gene.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Mutations in the gene encoding GLE1 cause an aggressive form of motor neuron disease.
items.explanation: Mutations in GLE1, which mediates mRNA export, are linked to an aggressive infantile motor neuron disease.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: The primary problem with ALS genes related to defective axonal cytoskeleton and transport is due to mutations in SOD1.
items.explanation: Defective axonal cytoskeleton and transport are associated with dynactin and profilin-1, not SOD1.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Survival was included as an endpoint in the study of edaravone's effects on ALS.
items.explanation: In the study of edaravone, survival was not included as an endpoint.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.statement: Tofersen is approved for use in all forms of ALS.
items.explanation: Tofersen is FDA approved specifically for SOD1-mediated ALS, not all forms.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

items.statement: Multifocal Motor Neuropathy with Conduction Block typically presents with corticospinal signs.
items.explanation: MMCB is not typically associated with corticospinal signs.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.statement: Infantile SMA has a slowly progressive course and patients often live beyond the first year.
items.explanation: Infantile SMA has a rapidly fatal course with death generally within the first year of life.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.statement: Primary Lateral Sclerosis can progress to a more aggressive course with lower motor neuron degeneration.
items.explanation: Infrequently, PLS may convert to a more aggressive course with lower motor neuron degeneration as in ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.statement: Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia is always inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.
items.explanation: HSP can be transmitted as an autosomal trait, but particularly when recessively inherited, it may have complex forms.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.assertion: In ALS, there is a progressive loss of both upper and lower motor neurons.
items.reason: The diagnosis of ALS is questionable in the absence of clear involvement of both motor neuron types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY
items.assertion: In ALS, there is proliferation of microglia and astrocytes.
items.reason: This phenomenon can be visualized using positron emission tomography (PET) scanning for ligands recognized by activated microglia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS | PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: ALS can present with pseudobulbar affect.
items.reason: Degeneration of corticobulbar projections results in exaggeration of motor expressions of emotion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: ALS is diagnosed when only one site shows upper and lower motor neuron involvement.
items.reason: A mutation in the gene encoding superoxide dismutase (SOD1) can be an exception to this rule.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ALS diagnosis requires more than one site involved unless there's a SOD1 mutation.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: ALS can present with cognitive impairment in some patients.
items.reason: Neuropsychological testing may detect subtle cognitive deficits in about 15% of clinically pure ALS cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ALS can present with cognitive impairment.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy is associated with deposition of TDP43 in motor neurons.
items.reason: This condition mimics ALS and involves neurofibrillary tangles in motor neurons.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association between chronic traumatic encephalopathy and TDP43 deposition.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Sporadic ALS may involve heritable factors.
items.reason: Motor neurons derived from stem cells of individuals with sporadic ALS show diminished viability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason supports the assertion about heritable factors in sporadic ALS.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Mutations in FUS/TLS lead to motor neuron cell death due to enhanced protein synthesis.
items.reason: FUS/TLS mutations may represent loss of function or toxic gain of function.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the passage does not link FUS/TLS mutations to enhanced protein synthesis; the reason is true.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Adult Tay-Sachs Disease can be distinguished from ALS due to its very slowly progressive nature.
items.reason: In Adult Tay-Sachs Disease, symptoms may have been present for years and the progression is slow.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.assertion: Juvenile SMA (SMA III) is characterized by weakness predominantly in distal muscles.
items.reason: In Juvenile SMA, weakness is greatest in the proximal muscles, not distal ones.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; weakness in Juvenile SMA is greatest in the proximal muscles.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED LOWER MOTOR NEURON DISORDERS

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.assertion: Primary Lateral Sclerosis is characterized by the selective loss of large pyramidal cells in the precentral gyrus.
items.reason: The degeneration of corticospinal and corticobulbar projections leads to this neuropathologic finding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON

items.subtopic: SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
items.assertion: The most common childhood-onset dominant form of Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia arises from mutations in the spastin gene.
items.reason: Mutations in the atlastin gene are responsible for the most common childhood-onset dominant form of HSP.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the most common childhood-onset dominant form arises from mutations in the atlastin gene, not spastin.
items.reference: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Other Motor Neuron Diseases · SELECTED DISORDERS OF THE UPPER MOTOR NEURON
